Although, strictly speaking, redox reactions involve the transfer of electrons, the most common form of redox reaction involves the exchange of hydrogen atoms between molecules (where the molecule has been oxidized, the reaction can therefore be further classified as a *dehydrogenation* reaction). In such cases, the reaction is still an example of a redox reaction because, every time a hydrogen atom leaves a molecule, it does so with an electron attached. For this reason, hydrogen atoms are often referred to as *reducing equivalents*, because they are equivalent to electrons (recall from Figure 3.1 that hydrogen atoms have a single proton and electron).

Redox reactions involving the transfer of hydrogen atoms are essentially the underpinning basis for exercise metabolism during endurance-type exercise, because it is the transfer of hydrogen atoms from the food we eat to oxygen molecules (to produce water) which produces the energy store (ATP) to fuel our muscles during exercise. In such circumstances, oxygen is said to be the final *electron acceptor*, as it undergoes a four-electron reduction (by accepting four hydrogen atoms) to produce water. Collectively, this process is known as *oxidative phosphorylation* and we will cover this in detail in Chapter 5.

## Definition

Redox (reduction-oxidation) reactions are reactions that, strictly speaking, involve the transfer of electrons between molecules. The most common biological form involves the exchange of hydrogen atoms between molecules; when a molecule loses a hydrogen atom (along with its electron), the reaction can be further classified as a *dehydrogenation* reaction.

## Core Principles

- Every time a hydrogen atom leaves a molecule, it does so with an electron attached, so hydrogen-atom transfer qualifies as electron transfer (a redox event).
- Hydrogen atoms are referred to as *reducing equivalents* because they are equivalent to electrons (a hydrogen atom comprises a single proton and a single electron).
- A molecule that loses hydrogen atoms/electrons is *oxidized*; the molecule that gains them is *reduced*.
- In aerobic metabolism, oxygen serves as the final *electron acceptor*, undergoing a four-electron reduction (by accepting four hydrogen atoms) to produce water.

## Sub-concepts

- *Dehydrogenation reactions*: a sub-class of redox reaction defined by the loss of hydrogen atoms from the oxidized molecule.
- *Reducing equivalents*: hydrogen atoms (proton + electron) used as the carrier currency of electron transfer in metabolism.
- *Final electron acceptor*: oxygen, which is reduced to water at the terminus of the electron transfer chain.
- *Oxidative phosphorylation*: the collective process whereby hydrogen atoms from food are transferred to oxygen to generate ATP (covered in detail in Chapter 5).

## Practical Applications

- Redox reactions involving hydrogen-atom transfer underpin exercise metabolism during endurance-type exercise: hydrogen atoms from ingested food are transferred to oxygen to produce water, generating the ATP that fuels skeletal muscle contraction.

## Related Concepts

- Reducing equivalents

- Oxidative phosphorylation
